operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+GSOC Idea Discussion (Concept Formation--Clustering) (14beefc1a4b0f073)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: GSOC Idea Discussion (Concept Formation--Clustering)
Messages: 2
Date range: 6 Mar 2015 to 2015-03-07
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14beefc1a4b0f073

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Vinshul
Date: 6 Mar 2015

Hi, I am Vinshul, a final year student at IIT-BHU. I am proficient in C++, Python and worked on parallel environments. I worked on a parallel version of Clustering Algorithm (K-Means) on Hadoop. I think I have sufficient background for Clustering the Atoms using different algorithms.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-07

Hi Vinshul,

As indicated on the Projects page, one approach to getting a variety of clustering algorithms working in OpenCog is described here:
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Clustering

Most of the work would be in dealing with the dimensional embedding code in OpenCog. Getting the dimensional embedding code running on some simple example would be a good start.

-- Ben G

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+GSOC MOSES the Pleasure Algorithm (14bb1654788654aa)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: GSOC MOSES the Pleasure Algorithm
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-02-22 to 2015-02-23
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14bb1654788654aa

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-02-22

Hi Devansh,



thanks for your interest.



I think that would be really cool, but I don't know how many slots we're 

gonna have for GSoC (if any), and that project isn't amongst the highest 

priorities, so it's really not sure there would be any funding for it. 

Otherwise, I'd be happy to mentor you on that.



So if you're still motivated, I can think of some bug for you to fix, to 

get familiar with the combo reduct code, and I can provide you feedback 

on your proposal before the final submission.



Nil

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-22

Hi Nil, Devansh,



Last year we were lucky enough to get a lot of slots; if we get as

many slots this year the odds of being able to fund a project on this

topic are fairly high I would say



-- Ben



On Sun, Feb 22, 2015 at 9:06 PM, Nil Geisweiller

<ngeiswei@googlemail.com> wrote:

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-22

Hey Nil,



Some subdirectory already contains pleasure code from years ago.  I suspect

it doesn't comiple. Its not documented probably  Probably no wiki pages for

it ?? and it doesn't have unit tests.   So these would be all good places

to start.



More generally, pleasure makes use of a notion of "repository of

previously-learnt combo proceedures that we can recycle for new learning

tasks".  I once glanced at that code as well, but its not clear its

well-designed, documented, tested, etc.  so that would be cool to have,

completely independent of pleasure



---linas



On Sun, Feb 22, 2015 at 7:06 AM, Nil Geisweiller <ngeiswei@googlemail.com>

wrote:

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Devansh shah
Date: 2015-02-23

Hi Nil & Ben,



I think it is best that I start getting familiar with the combo reduct code

and try solve a few bugs.



Would really appreciate if you could mentor me, and point me to a bug.



Regarding the SoC, maybe its best we talk about it once there is a clearer

picture?



Thanks,

Devans

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-02-23

Hi,



Linas, the project that Devansh suggests is different than PLEASURE, 

it's merely generalizing/refactoring the reduct engine to be more 

algebraic oriented.



PLEASURE has still a future of course, I guess it could be coded in 

AtomSpace-ish, possibly relying the pattern miner, etc.



Devansh, I'll get back to you soon with some initial guidance.



Nil

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+GSOC project - ROS proxy for OpenCog (14ac0d7408d8030d)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: GSOC project - ROS proxy for OpenCog
Messages: 9
Date range: 2015-01-06 to 2015-02-20
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ac0d7408d8030d

============================================================

--- Message 1/9 ---
From: Ankur Yadav
Date: 2015-01-06

I am a Computer Engineering student. I want to work on the ROS proxy for OpenCog for GSoC 2015.

--- Message 2/9 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-06

Three places where this can be done: replacing the current protocol used to drive Unity game engine characters with ROS, getting ROS to communicate between OpenPsi and eva_behavior, and improving the current robots code for various robots. The first two can be done without physical robots.

--- Message 3/9 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-06

Replacing Unity networking would be suitable for someone who likes game AI. Making OpenCog control specific robots using Gazebo simulations would also be a meaningful project.

--- Message 4/9 ---
From: Morgan Creekmore
Date: 2015-01-06

I am currently working on updating spock to work with the newest Minecraft version. I am hoping to write a plugin for spock that uses ROS to communicate with OpenCog.

--- Message 5/9 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-01-06

Excellent! Have you already seen this thesis from Jonas Kemper, who implemented a Spock interface to Minecraft for another AGI project MicroPsi?

--- Message 6/9 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-18

The github.com/opencog/ros consists entirely of docker files for running robots and Hanson Robotics heads, using blender for simulations not gazebo. Currently opencog has extremely poor 3D awareness, capability or dexterity. It does not know how to enter a room, look around, figure out where walls and doors are. It cannot recognize a table, roll up to it, and pick up an object. For this you need gazebo, not a game engine.

--- Message 7/9 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-19

The ros_opencog_robot_embodiment repo was created when Jamie and Shujing were starting work on using the planner to control a Turtlebot robot. This work was left off partway through. OpenCog can represent 3D environments using the 3DSpaceMap. I have suggested replacing its guts with an OctoMap.

--- Message 8/9 ---
From: Jamie Diprose
Date: 2015-02-20

I personally think OpenCog internal messaging system should be fixed before work on embodiment is done. That is what hindered me from finishing that project. Its not very transparent how to communicate with the embodiment module and its all done with ugly XML.

--- Message 9/9 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-02-20

We plan to have Hana work on replacing the current messaging system with something ROS-based. She will be in HK from mid/late March thru the end of June with this as one of her goals.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+GSoC student Interest plus Project Ideas (14beefc1a1dd050a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: GSoC student Interest plus Project Ideas
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-03-03 to 2015-03-07
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14beefc1a1dd050a

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Megan Barnes
Date: 2015-03-03

Hello, I am a prospective GSoC student interested in OpenCog. I have a background in language processing using Python and NLTK, so I was intrigued by the anaphora resolution project. I was wondering which NLP projects your organization is most interested in.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-07

Hi Megan,

Do you know C++ at all? The most ambitious/exciting NLP project for this summer probably involves helping Linas Vepstas with unsupervised language learning, but that requires C++ coding...

If C++ isn't your thing, there's a project involving customizing a rule-based comprehension pipeline to handle biomedical texts effectively.

-- Ben G

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Haskell bindingsreference resolution - GSoC 2015 (14be8c470ed0fbc3)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Haskell bindingsreference resolution - GSoC 2015
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-03-03 to 2015-03-08
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14be8c470ed0fbc3

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: Vinit Ravishankar
Date: 2015-03-03

Im interested in working on two projects: first, writing Haskell bindings - Ive worked in both Haskell and C++ before, and the project sounds interesting. [...]

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-05

Nil, Would you have time to mentor work on Haskell bindings? Vinit, We would like to have haskell bindings but have a very muddy, very poor idea about how they should work. You should know that the atomspace is a typed graphical database. Some of the types allow functions signatures (terms) to be specified, so the atomspace is a kind-of term algebra -- we use a variant of that language, called combo, for introspection and re-writing (reduction). We think that all of these ideas -- types, introspection, reduction, terms, should probably map well into haskell, but we dont really know how. The core object to represent is the atom.

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-05

The best guide for understanding the requirements of the Haskell bindings is to look at the Scheme bindings, I would say... In functionality terms, the place to start would be to replicate the Atom functions in the Scheme shell. The first question is whats the right way to get and put Atoms from/to the Atomspace using Haskell... Currently OpenCog Atoms are not explicitly typed, but Nil Geisweiler has been discussing adding a Haskell or Haskellish type system to the Atomspace.

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-05

Linas, you bet, its probably THE project Id like to mentor this year. Ben would be qualified as well but his resource is valuable for so many other things. Vinit, start studying the AtomSpace and its API. Try to demonstrate in your proposal that you understand how to proceed step-by-step with the task. Get back to me if you want me to review your proposal before submitting.

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Vinit Ravishankar
Date: 2015-03-05

Thanks for the advice - Ill get to reading the documentation and other stuff right away. Nil, do you mind if I mail you every now and then with questions and tentative proposals? I think constant feedback might help me a lot.

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-06

Continuing this topic is fine ;)

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-06

No problem

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Vinit Ravishankar
Date: 2015-03-08

Ive developed a general plan of action. Initially, I need to develop a way for Haskell to communicate with the AtomSpace API. Thered also need to be a way to represent the AtomSpace in Haskell. Further, Id have to make some basic types for Nodes and Links - maybe encapsulate them in a typeclass. [...]

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Haskell bindings EquivalenceLink Type Constraints (14fdfdee23709627)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Haskell bindings EquivalenceLink Type Constraints
Messages: 15
Date range: 18 Sep 2015 to 2015-09-25
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14fdfdee23709627

============================================================

--- Message 1/15 ---
From: Roman Treutlein
Date: 18 Sep 2015

Hello,

i am currently using the Haskell bindings to interface with the AtomSpace 
in my project that transforms Lojban into Atoms.
The following example is given on the Article for ConceptNode in the wiki:

 EquivalenceLink <http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/EquivalenceLink>
 [...]

--- Message 2/15 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-09-18

Hi Roman,

Haskell is right, although of course it all depends on the formal 
definition of EquivalenceLink which may vary overtime, it did use to be 
used in such a way, but not anymore.

You would have to use a SimilarityLink which works over concepts rather 
than predicates, but I think in this case you probably want to use 
DefineLink, although there is no DefinedConceptNode yet, I don't know if 
DefineLink would work.

Nil

--- Message 3/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-18

On Fri, Sep 18, 2015 at 7:10 AM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog <
opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

What a strange thing to say.  News to me!  When did it change? Where is the
authoritative reference?  Who made this decision? I based the design of
DefineLink on being the dual to equivalenceLink; if EquivalenceLink has
changed, then so must the DefineLink!

So ???  quite befuddled by the above remark.

DefineLink is the imperative dual of the EquivalenceLink. You use
EquivalenceLink if you want to declare a relation, the Define if you want
an imperative action to be taken. They're duals, like all the others.

http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/Link_comparison

--linas

--- Message 4/15 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-19

Hi Linas,

In PLN,

-- EquivalenceLink and ImplicationLink are used between predicates,

-- SimilarityLink and InheritanceLink are used between concepts

That is what it says here too

http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/EquivalenceLink

and I guess we all agree... no? ...

ben

--- Message 5/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-18

But its contradicated by
http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/SatisfyingSetLink  which has
this example:

 EquivalenceLink
     ConceptNode "the-set-of-things-that-breath-air"
     SatisfyingSetLink
         EvaluationLink
             PredicateNode "breathe"
             ListLink
                 VariableNode "$X"
                 ConceptNode "air"

It seems plausible that one wants to be able to say something like the
above ... if one cannot say it with EquivalenceLink, then how?

--linas

--- Message 6/15 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-19

That would be either a SimilarityLink (w/strength 1) or a
DefinedConceptLink (doesn't exist at the moment) ...

I'm not radically opposed to extending/overloading EquivalenceLink to
sets as well as functions, but I worry that might create (even more)
confusion...

--- Message 7/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-18

Hmm. OK. Problem is that the wiki page for
http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/SimilarityLink  is a cryptic.

Perhaps the breathes-air example should be
http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/ExtensionalSimilarityLink

A meta-problem is that the word "equivalence" in EquivalenceLink implies
something stronger than mere similarity: its strong and bold; while
SimilarityLink just sounds wishy-washy and indecisive when one wants to
talk about strong identity or definition.

--linas

--- Message 8/15 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-19

True...

We could overload EquivalenceLink for both cases, or rename
SimilarityLink as ConceptEquivalenceLink... or live with the
English-semantics issue...

--- Message 9/15 ---
From: Roman Treutlein
Date: 20 Sep 2015

Okay i tried using a Similarity link but that only accepts ConceptNodes a SatisfyingSetLink 
is not valid.
DefineLinks can not be used at all as there is no way to create one.

I have also noticed that SatisfyingSetLinks only take a PredicateNode. But 
 [...]

--- Message 10/15 ---
From: Roman Treutlein
Date: 20 Sep 2015

Also there are no constructors for ExtSimLink and IntSimLink just for the 
general SimLink.
But i will need the Extensional and Intensional Versions to differentiate 
le/lo.

le: non-veridical descriptor: the one(s) described as ... -Intensional 
 [...]

--- Message 11/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-21

Hi,

I think you need to work with Nil to get haskell set up as needed. You're a
bit on the bleeding edge, using some link types which are not used a lot
(thus the outdated/confusing wiki status) and also the bleeding edge
haskell bindings ...

--linas

On Sun, Sep 20, 2015 at 11:51 PM, Roman Treutlein <lordmord94@gmail.com>
wrote:

--- Message 12/15 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-09-24

Hi Roman,

That is because we still lack support for deep types 
http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/Deep_types.
You can work around that by relaxing the type constraints defined in

<ATOMSPACE_ROOT>/opencog/haskell/OpenCog/AtomSpace/Types.hs

See 'data Atom'.

Yes this type should be updated as well to follow the description in 
wiki page

http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/SatisfyingSetLink

the reason it is coded that way in the Haskell binding is probably 
because I disagree with the wiki page (well Marcos coded it, I don't 
know how from where he got it, maybe via telepathy, or maybe it made 
more sense to him as well ;-)

Basically Linas prefers to have variable scope definitions in multiple 
places like BindLink, SatisfyingSetLink, etc, while I would rather have 
 [.. [...]

--- Message 13/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-24

On Thu, Sep 24, 2015 at 1:08 AM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog <
opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

Well,  except that page is obsolete; it should probably be deleted. Nil, if
you recall, we had a long deep-types conversation, and worked out the
details, about half-a-year ago. I cut-n-pasted the emails into these two
wiki pages:

http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/Type_signature

and here:
http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/Type_checking

I don't think that statement is correct!

Since the only place where scoping is done is in the LambdaLink (and since
I wrote that code) I'm not sure what you are complaining about ...

And we do .. !!?? Can't guess what you mean here.

... and I don't understand that either ...

 [...]

--- Message 14/15 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-09-25

Linas,

Sure, these pages (Ben's deep type page and yours type signature and 
type checking pages) should be cleaned-up and unified. I did intend to 
serious tackle that problem but I just think getting a working URE first 
is more important right now.

That is true for the C++ code, but not for the user's end. Obviously 
we're in uncharted territory here, I'm not fully confident of what is 
right or wrong, which is why I'm not vehemently opposed to the way it 
currently is, as long as ambiguities are kept to a minimum.

Yes we can, but no we don't! Or at least not as often as I would like 
to. What I mean is that a SatisfyingSetLink would in my view take a 
predicate, nothing more. A BindLink, should take a predicate and a 
 [...]

--- Message 15/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-25

Hmm OK

On Fri, Sep 25, 2015 at 11:44 AM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog <
opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+How to add spacetime atom types in Cogserver python shell, and how to start C... (14e288ec6670ce28)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: How to add spacetime atom types in Cogserver python shell, and how to start C...
Messages: 6
Date range: 24 Jun 2015 to 2015-06-30
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e288ec6670ce28

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 24 Jun 2015

Hi, I meet two problems about cogserver:
1. I don't know how to add spacetime atom types in cogserver.
In before without using python in py-shell in cogserver, I can have spacetime atom types in opencog.atomspace.types if I:
from opencog.spacetime import SpaceServer,Timeserver
from opencog. [...]

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 26 Jun 2015

Hi,

I've partially solved the problem 1 by adding a function to refresh atom types, such as:

def get_refreshed_types():
    return type('atom_types', (), generate_type_module())

for problem2 is anyone can help?

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 26 Jun 2015

If you run the cogserver outside the build directory the search for modules starts from that directory and goes through its parent directories and certain other locations in search for defined modules/scheme-modules for loading, thus error.

you could possibly fix it by adding 2 flags (for scheme-modules and modules) for specifying alternate paths when starting the cogserver similar to -c flag for alternate configuration-file path.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 26 Jun 2015

Thanks for your reply Amen! I understand it clearer.
I would like to start cogserver outside since I think it's convenient for writing script. But that's also ok since I can just cd to the opencog/build

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-29

Unbound variable: cog-bind

The above can be fixed with

(use-modules (opencog query))

--linas

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-30

Because scheme is built into the cogserver, but the scheme-shell is a module. The port 18001 does not go through the cogserver, instead it goes straight into guile itself.

Hi, Sorry for the late response. Gmail did not deliver your email until just today.

You need to read about docker, learn what it is, and learn how to use it. Do some of their tutorials. Once you do this, the instructions won't seem so sketchy. The opencog docker files are plain and ordinary.

--linas

From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 11 Jan 2015

Refer to http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Building_OpenCog

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+How to control Minecraft embodiment (14f161697c91014c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: How to control Minecraft embodiment
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-08-09 to 2015-08-10
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f161697c91014c

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 2015-08-09

Now we have a perception manager, which can get visible blocks by calling ROS service. Im wondering whats the best way to control the working flow of Minecraft embodiment. [...]

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-10

Cool!!! Option 1 was my suggestion... Maybe Linas will explain if/why he thinks (1) is a bad idea... In principle, yes, threading should work... the Atomspace is thread-safe, and the octomap is thread-safe... However, it is not clear to me if there is some other code related to the 3DSpaceMap that is not thread-safe. I have asked Tesfa to look at thread-safety of the 3DSpaceMap related code once hes done with his current task...

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 2015-08-09

Actually I didnt consider this when I wrote the 3DSpaceMap... I will try to see if theres any problem in the next few days... But I think its better if there are experienced people can review the code.

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-10

The experienced people seem to be busy. So, it will likely be one of you clever inexperienced people to look at this issue (Tesfa is pretty good, but he just got his BS degree a couple months ago...)

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 2015-08-10

After spending one day on using py shell on cogserver and failed, I found that directly using python shell makes life much much happier. On the plain python shell now I can use opencog.bindlink to execute ExecutionOutputLink successfully to call Brads action function. [...]

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-10

There are multiple problems with option 1: The C++ code that handles mind-agents is single-threaded; the mind-agents have to run cooperatively. The python wrapper to the C++ mind-agents might be broken. Option 2 is really just a multi-threaded free-for-all. Any thread can do any thing at any time. Basically, a big part of threads is that its usually easier to use them, than to try to avoid them. Cooperative multi-tasking is just painful, error-prone. The atomspace is fully thread-enabled, so are the guile bindings. Python itself is only pseudo-thread-enabled due to the GIL.

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-10

Just be aware that, if you are not careful, you can end up working in a different atomspace than the one that the cogserver is working in. There are only a few reasons to use the cogserver: the REST interfaces need a running server, if you need to telnet to port 17001. So, no, you probably dont need to run the cogserver, I guess.

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 2015-08-10

OK Ill pay attention to this. Thats great to know that I can use the built-in python shell to work. I think I will try this until I find any problem.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+How to use bindlink correctly in Cplusplus (14e9ef105741e4b5)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: How to use bindlink correctly in Cplusplus
Messages: 4
Date range: 17 Jul 2015 to 28 Jul 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e9ef105741e4b5

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 17 Jul 2015

Hi, I'm revising the SpaceMap and need to take predicate info from atomspace using BindLink. But I got an empty SetLink instead of the expected result. Most use cases in tests are in Scheme. Is there anything wrong in my C++ code?

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-18

Hi YiShan,

Yes, it should work. I'm pretty sure I know what the problem is: the atomspace that python is using is not the same as the atomspace that the C++ code is using. There is some squirmy code to make sure that the two stay the same, but somehow, that keeps breaking.

This is a recurring bug/feature in the python bindings, and needs to be fixed.

--linas

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-07-27

Hi YiShan, keep in mind that your BindLink query can be simplified using GetLink. You don't need to specify the variables, all variables are bound by default.

Nil

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Yi-Shan Chen
Date: 28 Jul 2015

Hi Linas and Nil: I got it! I make a foolish naming mistake in the unit test code. And I've tried the get link in C++, that's very convenient. Thank you!

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+IfElseLink (14f36df514e5ca3f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: IfElseLink
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-08-16 to 2015-08-20
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f36df514e5ca3f

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-16

This has come up in Yi-Shan's Minecraft work. Any thoughts on adding IfElseLink with semantics: IfElseLink X Y Z is synonymous to ImplicationLink X Y plus ImplicationLink (NotLink X) Z?

PLN could deal with these simply by expanding them into ImplicationLinks using a single new rule...

-- Ben

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-20

The suggestion below touches on a bunch of old, unresolved issues. They are now easier to talk about, thanks to the invention of the PresentLink.

Another early confusion was whether BindLink was exactly the same thing as ImplicationLink. The answer is: for every important PLN link type, there is an equivalent PM link type. BindLink is the Kripke equivalent of ImplicationLink.

So, for something like IfElseLink, these confusions renew themselves. Should IfElse check the TV values, or simply for presence? How are bound variables handled?

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Image classification using Neural Nets (14b3e2bc9c277e03)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Image classification using Neural Nets
Messages: 2
Date range: 30 Jan 2015 to 2015-01-31
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b3e2bc9c277e03

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Saleem Ahmed
Date: 30 Jan 2015

Hi, I would like to start working on Using DeSTIN and SVM or Neural Nets to Effectively Classify Images. I have already been working on a project for the recognition of regional indian languages using various features and SVM classification.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-31

Hi,

Using neural nets to classify images is worthwhile but not necessarily in-scope for an OpenCog GSoC project. The reason we have been working with DeSTIN is that we have a clear idea of how to map patterns in DeSTIN's states into an OpenCog AtomSpace...

Using a neural net to classify images could be in-scope if there were a similarly clear idea about how to interface the NN's states with OpenCog. For instance, there is such a clear story for Conceptor Networks.

-- Ben G

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Improved hBOA - GSOC 2015 (14bf254e9c15a350)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Improved hBOA - GSOC 2015
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-03-06 to 2015-03-19
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14bf254e9c15a350

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Arley Ristar
Date: 2015-03-06

I am a masters student in CS focused on intelligent agents and machine learning at UFPE Brazil. I am interested in the Improved hBOA idea for MOSES. Several questions about how hBOA compares to hill-climbing in this context.

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-07

In a MOSES context, hBOA underperformed hill-climbing and was removed from the current codebase. Given the same number of fitness evaluations as hillclimbing, it tended to come to worse answers. The problem was that in most practical contexts, there was not enough data for hBOA to mine sufficient meaningful dependency information. We are open to other algorithms. We encourage you to write a paper on your GSoC work if it generates new research results.

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Arley Ristar
Date: 2015-03-16

If hill-climbing outperformed hBOA, I will change my focus to testing another algorithm. Have you considered using PSO or ABC to refine? Have you considered creating an interface to a C++ optimization library?

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-19

The number of knobs grows as expression trees become larger. The typical problem is to limit growth and aim at the most-likely trees. MOSES does really quite poorly in learning continuous expressions because it tries to learn constants one bit at a time. The total space becomes hopelessly large very quickly: if there are 500 knobs, then the total space is about 3^500.

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-19

Using an interface to a C++ optimization library is not an obviously terrible idea. We have thought about it but not done it. Do you have some specific optimization algorithm or library in mind?

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Improving Python Unit Test Coverage (14ba7db69a3f3f8e)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Improving Python Unit Test Coverage
Messages: 3
Date range: 20 Feb 2015 to 2015-02-21
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ba7db69a3f3f8e

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 20 Feb 2015

In fixing bug #1245, it appears that none of the existing test suites is explicitly calling or testing PythonEval. I'd like to extend coverage for the Python unit tests.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-20

The concept of opencog modules and of the cogserver in general, is on somewhat shifting ground. Most functionality is now accessible directly via python or scheme bindings, without having to run the server.

PythonEval.cc serves two purposes: providing a python interpreter inside the server, and supporting ExecutionOutputLink with GroundedSchemeNode py:...

It would probably be better to write a new stand-alone test called cython/PythonEvalUtest.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-21

Hi Curtis

4) There is no reason to make unit tests run fast. They should be readable-ish. Most importantly, they should check typical usage.

3) A Python make-king function would use atomspace.add_node and atomspace.add_link.

1) should work

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Infinite TV lookahead (151699fb7019ff1a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Infinite TV lookahead
Messages: 16
Date range: 2015-12-03 to 2015-12-04
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151699fb7019ff1a

============================================================

--- Message 1/16 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-03

Hi Ben,

what are the advantages of considering the lookahead parameter k? Why 
not just consider the case where k tends to infinity. That's one 
parameter less to tune and the k->inf seems to corresponds to what we 
ultimately want, the posterior distribution of p, the true probability 
of the event given the existing evidences.

If a finite k really is what we want, what typical values should it have 
and why? In the code I found 2 settings, k=2 in the indefinite TV code, 
and k=800 in the simple TV code. Obviously the values should be the same 
across all TV types, but what I find interesting is the choices, k=2 is 
very small, k=800 is very large and would probably corresponds to k->inf 
in behavior. This predates OpenCog, according to git.

Thanks,
Nil

--- Message 2/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

It's been a while since I played with this stuff.  But I recall that for
beta distributions, using b = .95, k=2 or 3 gave more intuitive behaviors
than k=800 or k approaching infinity....  This was based on fiddling with a
bunch of examples on pieces of paper that were finally trashed when I moved
out of my house in Maryland and moved to Hong Kong ;p

As I recall, k is a parameter that may need to be re-tuned for each
distribution type to give intuitively sensible behavior....  But for each
distribution type one has in one's "distribution library" one can probably
keep it constant for practical purposes...   (bear in mind the relation
between k and b is nonlinear and distribution-dependent...)

I think the k in the SimpleTV code is a different parameter, that is
probably the k in

 [...]

--- Message 3/16 ---
From: Matthew Ikle
Date: 2015-12-03

Looking at Chapter 6 again, letting k —> infinity would also correspond to the probability interval —> p is in [0,1], which, while true, becomes useless information.

—matt

--- Message 4/16 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-03

If b=1 yes, but not if b<1.

Nil

--- Message 5/16 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-03

Can't help but notice that the general shape of such a thing roughly
resembles arctan or the neural-net sigma function.

viz: when n is small, c is about zero. When n is large c is about 1.
Somewhere in the middle is a transistion from 0 to 1 which is kind-of the
whole point of using arctans or sigmas and the like.

Another popular choice

c = 0.5 * (1 + (n-k) / sqrt(n^2+k^2) )

popular because it takes not many cycles to compute.  but more than n/(n+k)
 but its shaped more nicely, the standard deviation is nicer.

--linas

--- Message 6/16 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-03

Can you recall what you mean by "sensible behavior"?

Oh, that explains!

Thanks,
Nil

--- Message 7/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

Linas -- yeah, I recall the c = n / (n+k) is theoretically motivated in
some settings, but the assumptions there are so many that they don't really
matter much ... maybe using a sigmoid is better in practice...

On Thu, Dec 3, 2015 at 10:27 PM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog <
opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

--- Message 8/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

On Thu, Dec 3, 2015 at 10:27 PM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog <
opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

I dimly recall that large k resulted in iterated inferences that had most
intervals expanded too close to [0,1] ...

--- Message 9/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

So then to avoid this, you change b and then something else goes wrong ;p
... Matt might remember better..

--- Message 10/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

In general, the challenge is to keep the probability intervals from
spreading too close to [0,1] after a few inferences.... (representing the
fact that iterated inference actually does kill most of your knowledge ;-p )

--- Message 11/16 ---
From: Matthew Ikle
Date: 2015-12-03

OK — I have trying to recreate the problem here, but it will obviously take more time than I thought. I will work on this again tonight or this weekend to see if I can recall and/or recreate exactly what the problem was.

—matt

--- Message 12/16 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-03

Excellent! Thanks!

I'm also trying to plot stuff and gather understanding, but anything 
that can speed that up is welcome.

Nil

--- Message 13/16 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-04

Also, do you still have something related to that comment, page 130 
right before Subsection 6.1.1

"
Getting back to the “Bayesianism versus frequentism” issues raised in 
chapter
4, if one wished to avoid the heuristic assumption of decomposability 
regarding
! plausibilities involved in the premises, one would replace the first-
the first-order
order distributions assumed in Step Two with Dirac delta functions, 
meaning that
no variation around the mean of each premise plausibility would be 
incorporated.
This would also yield a generally acceptable approach, but would result 
in overall
narrower conclusion probability intervals, and we believe would in most 
cases
represent a step away from realism and robustness.
"

Did you experiment Beta vs Dirac? To me it seems that using a Dirac 
 [...]

--- Message 14/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

Wow that was a long time ago ;p ...

On Thu, Dec 3, 2015 at 11:39 PM, 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog <
opencog@googlegroups.com> wrote:

--- Message 15/16 ---
From: Matthew Ikle
Date: 2015-12-04

This is a question for Ben — I did not experiment with Dirac delta functionals, but Ben wrote this section.

—matt

--- Message 16/16 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-04

Well I don't have that data anymore.  But it seems to me that for nearly
all real-world data (which is messy) the beta assumption will be more
realistic than Dirac...
